:mod:`The Response class` 
=========================

.. module:: services.response

.. class:: Response(object)

    The **Response** implements some utilities to send an http response to a request.
    It is a semi-abstract class, it cannot be used as such, a subclass needs to be
    written (see :mod:`Responses`)

Public methods
--------------

    .. method:: setRequest(request)

    	:param request: name of the service
    	:type request: twisted.web.http.Request

    	Attaches a request to the current Response object

    .. method:: send_http_response(response,code)
    	
    	:param response: an arbitrary Python structure to be encoded into json and sent back to the client.
    	:param code: http code to attach to the response (e.g. 200 is ok).
    	:type code: int

    	Encodes the data to be sent, sets some http request header values and finishes the request by calling twisted.web.http.Request.finish
    	
    .. method:: send(response,code):

    	Same as :meth:`send_http_response` but forces the http code in the request to ok (200).

    .. method:: __call__(service,execute,*args,**kargs)

    	:param service: is set to True if we are executing a service through POST, to false if it is a page being served through GET.
    	:type service: Boolean
    	:param execute: any callable function, bound method or callable object to call.
    	:type execute: callable
    	
    	This method is not implemented, it raises a SystemError. This is the method subclasses
    	should override to implement a response. The arguments are sent to the callable 
    	*execute*. A simple implementation would be to call the callable straight away and
    	ignore errors::

    		from twisted.web import http

    		class DirectResponse(Response):

    			def __call__(service,execute,*args,**kargs):
    				result = execute(*args,**kargs)
    				self.send_http_response(result,http.OK)
